skill.name: voice-agents
skill.description: Build production-ready voice AI agents with natural conversation flow, low-latency audio processing, and effective turn-taking detection for seamless human-AI voice interactions.
skill.summary: Expert guidance for architecting voice AI agents using speech-to-speech or pipeline architectures with optimal latency and natural conversation patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill contains only documentation and architectural guidance for voice AI development. Static analysis flagged markdown backticks as shell commands and text patterns as cryptographic code, but all findings are false positives. No executable code, network calls, or security risks present.

content.user_title: Build natural voice AI agents with optimal latency
content.value_statement: Creating voice agents that feel natural requires understanding latency budgets and conversation dynamics. This skill provides proven patterns for speech-to-speech and pipeline architectures used in production systems handling millions of calls.
actual_capabilities: Design speech-to-speech architectures using OpenAI Realtime API for lowest latency
Implement pipeline architectures with separate STT, LLM, and TTS components
Configure voice activity detection for accurate turn-taking
Apply barge-in detection to handle user interruptions naturally
Optimize response length and format for spoken delivery
Handle noise and STT errors gracefully in production
limitations: Does not provide actual audio processing code or API integrations
Requires external STT, TTS, and LLM services for implementation
Latency targets depend on specific service providers and infrastructure
Best suited for developers building voice agents, not end users

use_cases.title: Customer Support Voice Agent
use_cases.description: Build a voice AI that handles customer inquiries with natural conversation flow, detecting when customers interrupt or need clarification.

use_cases.title: Voice-Enabled Productivity Assistant
use_cases.description: Create a hands-free AI assistant for professionals who need to interact while driving, cooking, or performing other tasks.

use_cases.title: Accessibility Voice Interface
use_cases.description: Design voice-first interfaces for users with visual impairments or mobility limitations who benefit from spoken interaction.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Voice Agent Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a voice agent architecture for a customer service use case. Include STT, LLM, and TTS components with latency estimates for each.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with voice agent architecture

prompt_templates.title: Latency Budget Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a latency budget for a voice agent targeting sub-500ms response time. Identify the maximum allowable latency for each component and potential optimization strategies.

prompt_templates.title: Turn-Taking Detection Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a voice activity detection system that handles barge-in (user interruptions) without cutting off the user mid-sentence. Include semantic understanding of when interruptions are appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Production Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Create error handling strategies for common voice agent failures: STT misrecognition, LLM timeout, TTS failure, and network interruptions. Include fallback behaviors and user-facing messages.

output_examples.input: Design a voice agent for restaurant reservations
output: Architecture: Pipeline (STT → LLM → TTS) for maximum control over reservation data
Latency budget: STT 200ms, LLM 800ms, TTS 300ms = 1300ms total
VAD: Use semantic detection to distinguish reservation details from background noise
Response format: Constrain to structured confirmation with name, time, party size

output_examples.input: How do I handle users who interrupt mid-response?
output: Implement barge-in detection that monitors audio during TTS playback
When interruption detected: immediately pause TTS, process user utterance
If interruption is question: answer directly, then resume or skip remaining content
If interruption is new topic: acknowledge and transition to new topic

best_practices: Measure and budget latency for each component - aim for under 500ms for natural conversation
Use semantic voice activity detection instead of silence-only detection to avoid false positives
Constrain response length in prompts to 2-3 sentences for natural spoken delivery
anti_patterns: Ignoring latency budget - the sum of all component latencies determines conversation naturalness
Silence-only turn detection - causes false positives from background noise and misses quick responses
Long responses - users cannot retain long spoken information; keep responses brief and structured

faq.question: What is the difference between speech-to-speech and pipeline architectures?
faq.answer: Speech-to-speech models like OpenAI Realtime API process audio directly with lowest latency and preserved emotion but offer less control. Pipeline architectures separate STT, LLM, and TTS for maximum control at each step but add latency from component handoffs.

faq.question: What latency target should I aim for?
faq.answer: Target under 500ms end-to-end latency for natural conversation feel. Above 800ms feels noticeably delayed. Below 300ms feels instantaneous but is difficult to achieve with pipeline architectures.

faq.question: How do I handle background noise in voice agents?
faq.answer: Use voice activity detection with noise suppression, implement semantic understanding to filter non-speech sounds, and design prompts that help the LLM distinguish relevant speech from noise artifacts.

faq.question: What is barge-in detection and why is it important?
faq.answer: Barge-in detection allows users to interrupt the AI mid-response, just like human conversations. Without it, users must wait for the AI to finish speaking, creating unnatural interactions and frustration.

faq.question: How do I handle STT errors and misrecognitions?
faq.answer: Implement confidence scoring to detect uncertain transcriptions, design prompts asking the LLM to identify unclear input, and create graceful clarification flows that confirm understanding before acting.

faq.question: Which Claude tools support voice agent integration?
faq.answer: Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can all assist with voice agent architecture and prompt design. For actual audio processing, integrate with external APIs like OpenAI Realtime API, ElevenLabs, or Google Cloud Speech.
